title: New Economic Policy (NEP) introduced
description: Tenth Party Congress replaces War Communism's grain requisitioning with a tax in kind and permits limited private trade and small-scale enterprise, reviving the economy after the civil war.

title: First Five-Year Plan launched
description: Stalin's plan for rapid state-directed industrialization goes into operation (formally adopted April 1929), prioritizing heavy industry over consumer goods and ending the NEP's mixed economy.

title: Wholesale collectivization of agriculture ordered
description: Central Committee resolution orders mass collectivization of peasant farms into kolkhozes and the 'liquidation of the kulaks as a class'; targets are repeatedly accelerated through 1930 amid violent peasant resistance.

title: Soviet famine of 1931-34
description: Forced collectivization and grain requisitioning trigger mass starvation across Ukraine, Kazakhstan, the North Caucasus and the Volga region; Britannica estimates at least 5 million deaths Soviet-wide, with death-toll and intent (including the 'Holodomor' framing specific to Ukraine) still debated among historians.

title: German invasion triggers total-war economy
description: Operation Barbarossa forces wholesale mobilization of Soviet industry, including evacuation of factories east of the Urals; the war effort costs an estimated 27 million Soviet lives and devastates the western USSR's capital stock.

title: Kosygin economic reform announced
description: Premier Alexei Kosygin outlines a decentralizing reform at the Central Committee Plenum, replacing many mandatory output targets with profit and sales indicators for enterprises; implementation stalls after the 1968 Prague Spring hardens conservative resistance.

title: Soviet invasion of Afghanistan
description: Soviet troops invade to prop up the communist government in Kabul, beginning a nine-year war the CIA estimated had cost roughly 18 billion rubles by 1986 (on the order of $1-2bn/year), straining the Soviet budget through the 1980s.

title: Gorbachev launches perestroika
description: Newly elected General Secretary Mikhail Gorbachev's April Plenum speech introduces 'uskoreniye' (acceleration) and lays the groundwork for perestroika (restructuring) and glasnost (openness), the reform program that restructures the Soviet economy over the following six years.

title: Dissolution of the USSR — Russia inherits the Soviet economy
description: The Soviet Union formally dissolves into 15 successor states (see GLOBAL entry); the Russian Federation inherits most Soviet economic institutions, foreign debt, and the UN Security Council seat, and begins market transition weeks later.

title: Gaidar 'shock therapy' price liberalization
description: Acting PM Yegor Gaidar's government frees prices on most goods overnight, ending Soviet-era price controls; consumer prices rise roughly 2,500% over 1992 as pent-up shortages give way to hyperinflation.
source_name: IMF Working Paper 92/92 — Price Liberalization in Russia: The Early Record

title: Voucher privatization program begins
description: Deputy PM Anatoly Chubais's program issues privatization vouchers (nominal value 10,000 rubles) to 144 million citizens to buy shares in state enterprises, the centerpiece of Russia's mass-privatization drive that gave rise to the 1990s oligarchs.

title: Russian financial crisis — ruble default
description: Government of PM Sergei Kiriyenko abandons its ruble defense, defaults on domestic GKO debt, and imposes a 90-day moratorium on external commercial debt payments; the ruble falls from about 6.3 to 21 per dollar within weeks.
source_name: Congressional Research Service — The Russian Financial Crisis of 1998

title: Putin inaugurated president; 2000s oil-boom growth begins
description: Vladimir Putin's inauguration marks the start of a decade of roughly 7% average annual GDP growth, fueled by rising oil and gas prices and post-1998-devaluation competitiveness, before growth slows after the 2008 crisis.

title: First Western sanctions over Crimea
description: US Executive Order 13660 authorizes sanctions on individuals and entities undermining Ukraine's sovereignty; coordinated US/EU/Canada sanctions follow on March 17, a day before Russia formally annexes Crimea.
source_name: Council of the European Union — Timeline of EU Sanctions Against Russia

title: Annexation of Crimea
description: Russia formally incorporates Crimea and Sevastopol following a disputed referendum, triggering the first major post-Cold War Western sanctions regime against Russia.

title: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
description: Russian forces invade Ukraine on multiple fronts (see GLOBAL entry), triggering the largest European war since 1945 and the most extensive Western sanctions regime ever imposed on a major economy.

title: SWIFT disconnection and central-bank reserve freeze
description: US, EU, UK and Canada remove major Russian banks from SWIFT messaging and freeze roughly $300bn of Russian central bank foreign reserves, the most severe financial sanctions ever applied to a G20 economy.

title: October Revolution — Bolsheviks seize power
description: Lenin's Bolsheviks depose Russia's Provisional Government and proclaim Soviet power, the founding event of the state economy that would dominate this timeline for the next 74 years.

title: War Communism — large-scale industry nationalized
description: Council of People's Commissars decree nationalizes all large-scale industrial enterprises (coal, iron and copper-ore mining and similar sectors), formalizing the War Communism" program of nationalized industry and forced grain requisitioning that lasts until the 1921 New Economic Policy."

title: USSR formally established
description: Congress of Soviets of Russia, Ukraine, Belarus and the Transcaucasian SFSR approves the Treaty on the Creation of the USSR, establishing the federal structure — including unified citizenship and centralized budget authority — that governs the Soviet economy until 1991.
source_name: US Library of Congress — Law Library of Congress blog

title: Second Five-Year Plan launched
description: Continuing the First Plan's priorities, the second plan (1933-37) again favors heavy industry; gross industrial production more than doubles over the period, though coal and oil targets are missed.

title: 1947 currency reform and end of wartime rationing
description: Government exchanges old-pattern rubles for new ones at 10:1 and abolishes the wartime rationing system in favor of uniform reduced state retail prices, the first postwar normalization of the Soviet consumer economy.
source_name: Seventeen Moments in Soviet History (Michigan State University)

title: Death of Stalin
description: Stalin dies after a stroke, triggering a Communist Party leadership struggle from which Nikita Khrushchev emerges, opening the door to the Thaw" and eventual de-Stalinization of economic administration."

title: Sovnarkhoz reform — regional economic decentralization
description: Khrushchev's address to the Supreme Soviet abolishes most all-Union industrial ministries and creates roughly 105 regional economic councils (sovnarkhozy) to decentralize planning, reversed in 1965 after proving difficult to coordinate.

title: 1961 ruble redenomination
description: Fifth Soviet monetary reform redenominates the ruble 10:1 and devalues it against the US dollar; the resulting ruble remains in circulation for over 37 years, the longest-lived Soviet currency incarnation.
source_name: Wikipedia — Monetary reform in the Soviet Union, 1961 (cross-check)

title: August Coup attempt against Gorbachev
description: Communist hardliners (KGB, military, party conservatives) briefly seize power to block a new union treaty and preserve central planning; the coup's collapse fatally undermines Gorbachev and accelerates the USSR's dissolution four months later.

title: 1993 constitutional crisis
description: Yeltsin's decree dissolving parliament triggers a ten-day standoff and armed clashes that kill over 180 people before tanks shell the White House; the crisis clears the way for Yeltsin's market-reform-entrenching December 1993 constitution.

title: Loans-for-shares privatization auctions
description: Commercial banks lend the cash-strapped government roughly $800m against collateral stakes in major state firms (Norilsk Nickel, Yukos, Sibneft and others); the government's 1996 default on the loans transfers these assets to bank owners at deep discounts, creating Russia's first generation of oligarchs.

title: Yukos affair — Khodorkovsky arrested
description: FSB agents arrest Yukos CEO Mikhail Khodorkovsky, then Russia's richest man, on tax-evasion and fraud charges; Yukos is later dismantled and its assets absorbed into state-controlled Rosneft, marking a turning point toward renewed state control of strategic industry under Putin.

title: Stabilization Fund of Russia established
description: Government creates a sovereign wealth fund to accumulate oil revenue earned above a cutoff price (initially $20/barrel Urals), insulating the budget from commodity swings after the 1998 crisis exposed the danger of oil-revenue dependence.
source_name: Wikipedia — Stabilization Fund of the Russian Federation (cross-check)

title: Russo-Georgian War and 2008 financial crisis pressure
description: War with Georgia accelerates capital flight and ruble weakness just as the global financial crisis hits; the central bank spends over $200bn in reserves defending the ruble through early 2009 as the stock market falls roughly 70% from its May peak.
source_name: Wikipedia — 2008 Russian financial crisis / Great Recession in Russia (cross-check)

title: "Black Tuesday" ruble collapse
description: Ruble falls nearly 20% in a single day to a record low near 80/dollar despite an emergency central-bank rate hike from 10.5% to 17%, driven by collapsing oil prices, Western sanctions over Crimea, and a Rosneft bailout absorbing dollar liquidity.

title: Russia's first foreign-currency sovereign default since 1918
description: Russia misses roughly $100m in Eurobond coupon payments after US sanctions block the payment channel, its first default on foreign-currency debt in over a century — a technical default caused by sanctions rather than an inability to pay.

title: Russian Empire enters World War I
description: Russia's general mobilization and Germany's subsequent declaration of war (1 August 1914) draw the empire into a war that conscripts roughly 15 million men, disrupts grain exports and rail-based supply chains, and drives runaway inflation and urban food shortages by 1916-17 -- economic strains that fed directly into the food riots and troop mutinies of the February and October 1917 revolutions (see separate entries).
